1:
The chapter opens far to the south, and the witnesses gather first around the queen herself — her country, her errand, and the "hard questions" she carried. The grammatical-historical Ellicott is most careful with the name. "Sheba" must be distinguished from Seba, the Cushite race linked with Egypt and Ethiopia; the kingdom in view lay in Arabia Felix, home of the Joktanite line, so that this queen was "of Semitic race, not wholly an alien from the stock of Abraham" (compare Gen. 10:28). He suspects it is the very confusion of Sheba with Saba that led Josephus to make her a "queen of Egypt and Ethiopia." The reformed Gill agrees that the metropolis of Arabia Felix is meant, and gathers the traditions — her Ethiopic name Maqueda, her Arabic name Belequis, and the guess that "the queen of the south" answers to an Arabic "Queen Teiman." He supposes she heard Solomon's fame through the ambassadors who frequented his court.

On the object of her journey the puritan Matthew Henry writes most warmly. She came not to treat of trade or alliance, as crowned heads commonly meet, but to satisfy her curiosity, to receive instruction, and — chiefly — because she had heard of Solomon "concerning the name of the LORD." Being religiously inclined, she desired to be better acquainted with the God whom Solomon worshipped and from whom he had his wisdom; and Henry notes that our Saviour makes her enquiries after God an aggravation of the stupidity of those who will not enquire after God by Christ, who is greater than Solomon (Matt. 12:42). The wesleyan Clarke fixes the same title — "queen of the south" — and reads the name Saba or Azab as itself meaning "the south."

The one point of friendly dispute concerns the "hard questions." Clarke and Gill take them as riddles, parables, and enigmas, the Septuagint's ainigmata, in which the wisdom of the East delighted and delights still. Ellicott presses higher: the puerile puzzles preserved in the Arabian legends and the Koran are unworthy of mention; the real "hard questions" must have been "those enigmatic and metaphorical sayings" in which Eastern philosophy embodied its deepest moral and speculative problems — the very matters handled in Job, Proverbs, and Ecclesiastes. In solving these, not fantastic ingenuity but genuine wisdom would be worthily employed. The witnesses converge on the chapter's opening note: a Gentile queen, drawn by report across a desert, comes seeking not commerce but wisdom, and behind the wisdom, the name of the LORD.
2:
Now the queen arrives "with a very great train," and the witnesses attend to her caravan, her gifts, and the riddles she "communed" to Solomon "of all that was in her heart." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch correct a natural misreading: the word rendered "train" (chayil) does not mean a military escort but riches — her numerous retinue of servants and her camels laden with treasure. What she "had upon her heart," they insist, is "whatever riddles she had it in her mind to lay before him"; there is "no ground for thinking of sayings of a religious nature, as the earlier commentators supposed," but simply of dark sayings whose solution would prove a very deep wisdom — and Solomon "was able to solve all her riddles."

On the cargo the grammatical-historical Ellicott supplies the background. The "spices" of Arabia were famous in every age; Sheba is named as trafficking with Tyre "in chief of all spices, and precious stones, and gold" (Ezek. 27:22). Frankincense for sacrifice and myrrh for embalming would be most in request (compare Isa. 60:6; Jer. 6:20); the gold and precious stones were either native to Sheba or drawn from the farther East. The wesleyan Clarke heaps up the classical testimony — Virgil's "effeminate Sabaeans" with their frankincense, Pliny on their odorous woods, and the ancient notice that the Sabaeans were governed by queens rather than kings — as evidence for placing her among that wealthy Arabian people. The reformed Gill simply pictures the scene: she came "with many of her courtiers and principal men, as well as with a large retinue of servants."

The single difference among them is one of judgement, not heat: whether the matters "in her heart" were religious enquiries, as the older expositors held, or the metaphysical riddles Keil and Delitzsch prefer. Even here the schools are not far apart, for the wisdom that could unlock either kind was, in Solomon, the gift of God. What all the witnesses see in the verse is the lavishness of a seeker: she spared no expense and hid nothing back, but "spake to Solomon all that was in her heart," resolved to lay her whole mind open to the wisest of men and to learn whether the half of what she had heard was true.
3:
The verse is brief — "Solomon told her all her questions: there was not any thing hid from the king, which he told her not" — but the witnesses find in it the perfect answer to the perfect test. The reformed Gill dwells on the completeness: Solomon "answered them, told her the meaning of everything she inquired about, expounded her riddles, solved her objections, and gave her satisfaction in all things she proposed unto him." Nothing was too dark for him and nothing too deep; the fame that had crossed the desert is here made good in a single sentence.

The wesleyan Clarke sets the exchange in its cultural world. "Riddles, problems, fables, apologues, etc., formed the principal part of the wisdom of the East"; the peoples of those lands "use and delight in them to the present day." He points back to Samson's wager over his riddle as an instance of the same taste for the enigmatic contest (Judg. 14:12; 14:14), so that the queen's questions were no idle amusement but the recognised proving-ground of a sage.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the emphatic negative — "there was not any thing hid from the king" — and then let the narrative move: having exhausted her riddles and found Solomon equal to every one, the queen turns from what she hears to what she sees, for the next verses gather the house, the table, and the ascent to the temple that will take away her breath. The puritan Matthew Henry, whose exposition runs across these verses, marks the range of the answers: whether her questions were "natural, moral, political, or divine," Solomon "gave her a satisfactory answer to all," making "things surprisingly easy which she apprehended insuperably difficult," so that she was "satisfied that there was a divine sentence in the lips of this king" (compare Prov. 16:10). The point they all fasten on is that Solomon's wisdom was not a matter of a few clever answers but a fulness that left no question standing. Where earthly reputation almost always shrinks under examination, here it held; the king told her all, and the telling prepared her to marvel at everything else. It is the note on which the whole visit will turn — that with Solomon the truth exceeded the report, and the exceeding began with his wisdom before ever it reached his wealth.
4:
Two things now begin the list of wonders — "all Solomon's wisdom, and the house that he had built" — and the witnesses read the verse as the moment report gives way to sight. The reformed Gill notes how the wisdom was "seen": she "perceived it by his answers to things relative to all sorts of science, natural, civil, and divine." It was not an abstract reputation but a demonstrated mastery, ranging over every field she could put to the test. The wesleyan Clarke agrees that she had seen Solomon's wisdom "by the answers which he gave to her subtle questions," and takes the house that so struck her to be "most probably his own house" — the royal palace rather than the temple.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the identification and refine it: the house she saw was "Solomon's palace, not the temple," the great structure of thirteen years' building (compare 1 Kings 7:1). From the building the historian's eye will move at once to the table, the officers, the servants, and the ascent to the house of the LORD — the whole ordered magnificence of the court. What the witnesses stress in this verse is the pairing of the two objects. First named is the wisdom, and only after it the architecture; for though the palace was a marvel of the age, it was the mind behind it that the queen had truly come to see. The building impressed the eye, but the wisdom had impressed the world, and the historian keeps them in that order. She had crossed a wilderness on the strength of a rumour; now the rumour stood before her in stone and in speech alike, and the sight would prove, as the next verse tells, more than she could bear. The verse thus turns the hinge of the visit: the testing of the riddles is over, and the overwhelming of the senses is about to begin. The historian has set the two together on purpose — the invisible wisdom first, then the visible house — so that the reader, like the queen, may learn to prize the mind above the masonry.
5:
The catalogue of splendour reaches its climax and its breaking-point: the food of the table, the seat of the servants, the attendance and apparel of the ministers, the cup-bearers, and the ascent by which the king went up to the house of the LORD — "there was no more spirit in her." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold the list item by item: the servants are the chief officers, counsellors, and aides; the ministers the court attendants; the "sitting" and "standing" are the places in the palace assigned to each rank, all "contrived with wisdom and arranged in a splendid manner," down to the clothing they received from the king and the whole ordered arrangement of his drink. The reformed Gill points to the daily provision of the table, its "various sorts" and multitude of dishes (compare 1 Kings 4:22).

The one genuine dispute concerns the last clause. The wesleyan Clarke thinks it "very strange that the steps to the temple should be such a separate matter of astonishment," and observes that the Hebrew is rendered by the Vulgate, Septuagint, Chaldee, Syriac, and Arabic — and by Luther — as "the burnt-offerings which he offered in the house of the LORD." On that reading it was Solomon's princely sacrifices and pious fervour, not a stairway, that overwhelmed her (compare 1 Kings 8:63). The puritan Matthew Henry keeps both senses in view: he reads first "the ascent by which he went up to the house of the LORD," the gravity and devotion of the king's approach to worship, and then adds that "many of the ancient versions read it, The burnt-offerings which he offered." Keil and Delitzsch and Gill hold the received "ascent," but all agree that the crown of the list is Solomon's religion: the thing that finished her wonder was the sight of so much greatness bending in worship before a greater God.

On the effect the witnesses are one. Clarke reads "there was no more spirit in her" as a real swoon — "she was overpowered with astonishment; she fainted" — and compares it to the natural effect of rare and extraordinary things upon a "delicate sensible mind." The queen who had crossed a desert to test a report is here undone by it; the half had not been told her, and the sight of it emptied her of speech. What began as a contest of riddles ends as an act of amazement, and the verse leaves her, appropriately, without a word — until the next verse gives her one.
6:
Recovering her breath, the queen speaks, and her first word is an honest confession that rumour, for once, had not lied: "It was a true report that I heard in mine own land of thy acts and of thy wisdom." The reformed Gill catches the moment exactly — she said this "when she was a little come to herself," the astonishment of the previous verse giving way at last to speech. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill out the sense: she says "with astonishment" that of what her eyes now saw she had not heard the half, and that the report which had reached her in her own land, and had "hitherto appeared incredible to her," was now proved true.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the character of the woman from the admission. She is "far from repenting her journey or calling herself a fool for undertaking it"; rather she acknowledges "it was well worth her while to come so far for the sight of that which she could not believe the report of." Henry turns it to a general observation about human report and expectation: usually things are "represented to us, both by common fame and by our own imagination, much greater than we find them when we come to examine them; but here the truth exceeded both fame and fancy." It is the rare case in which acquaintance enlarges rather than shrinks the reputation.

The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the turning of a sceptic into a witness: the queen who had come to prove Solomon now stands as a voucher for him, and her testimony is the more weighty because she had doubted. Her acts and his wisdom — the two things she names — are precisely the two she had set out to test, and on both counts she declares the report vindicated. What she had carried across the wilderness as a rumour she carries home as knowledge, and the verse marks the exact point at which hearing passed into sight and sight into confession. Nothing in Solomon's court had been magnified in the telling; if anything, the telling had fallen short.
7:
The confession deepens into wonder: "Howbeit I believed not the words, until I came, and mine eyes had seen it: and, behold, the half was not told me." The reformed Gill is careful about the measure of her earlier unbelief. She had not disbelieved the whole — "somewhat of it she credited, and supposed there was something grand and extraordinary in it, or she would never have taken such a journey" — but she thought the reports "too much magnified," that things had been overstated. The event rebukes her caution: the reality outran even the extravagant rumour.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the verse forward into its overflow: not content with owning that his wisdom and prosperity "exceedeth the fame," she goes on to congratulate his servants, who stand continually before him and hear his wisdom, and then to bless Jehovah his God. The astonishment does not stop at the king but reaches to those near him and up to the God who gave him.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the verse to its spiritual use. Here, uniquely, "the truth exceeded both fame and fancy"; and "those who, through grace, are brought to experience the delights of communion with God will say that the one-half was not told them of the pleasures of Wisdom's ways." He presses it further still: "Glorified saints, much more, will say that it was a true report which they heard of the happiness of heaven, but that the thousandth part was not told them" (1 Cor. 2:9). The queen's amazement becomes a parable of every soul that comes to Christ and finds him better than his fame.

The witnesses converge on the note that the report, so far from exaggerating, had undersold the reality. It is the exact reverse of the world's ordinary experience, where anticipation flatters and possession disappoints; with Solomon — and, the witnesses would add, far more with the Solomon who is greater than Solomon — the seeker's highest expectation is the least part of what is found. Gill's careful point about her measured earlier doubt only sharpens the reversal: she who had thought the reports "too much magnified" now finds no words large enough, and her very caution becomes the measure of how far the reality outran the fame.
8:
Still marvelling, the queen turns to those who serve at Solomon's side: "Happy are thy men, happy are these thy servants, which stand continually before thee, and that hear thy wisdom." The reformed Gill reads it of "the men of Israel, that had a king over them so wise, so great, so good" — a whole people blessed in their sovereign, and above them the near attendants who drank in his wisdom day by day. The wesleyan Clarke adds only that these are "very natural expressions from a person in her state of mind," the overflow of a heart too full to keep silent.

The puritan Matthew Henry draws out what lay behind the beatitude. She "pronounced those happy that constantly attended him, and waited on him at table," reckoning that "they may improve their own wisdom by hearing his." Indeed, Henry says, "she was tempted to envy them, and to wish herself one of them." From this he takes a plain lesson: "It is a great advantage to be in good families, and to have opportunity of frequent converse with those that are wise, and good, and communicative," a happiness "many have who know not how to value it."

And Henry lifts the saying, as the queen could not, to its higher object: "With much more reason may we say this of Christ's servants, Blessed are those that dwell in his house, they will be still praising him" (Ps. 84:4). The witnesses do not divide. They hear in the queen's words the instinct of a true seeker, who counts nearness to wisdom the greatest of privileges and envies not the king's gold but the servants' daily hearing of his wisdom — a nearness the least believer enjoys toward one wiser than Solomon. The gold and the throne had struck her senses; but it was the men who stood in the light of that wisdom whom she called happy, and in this she judged better than she knew. Henry's application only makes explicit what the queen's instinct had grasped: that the truest riches of a great court are not its treasures but its daily converse with wisdom, and that to dwell where such wisdom is heard is a happiness worth more than a throne. She envied the servants, not the king's gold; the seeker's eye had found the better part.
9:
The queen's praise rises at last to its true source: "Blessed be the LORD thy God, which delighted in thee, to set thee on the throne of Israel: because the LORD loved Israel for ever, therefore made he thee king, to do judgment and justice." The reformed Gill notes that she now had "better notions" of the God of Israel than when she left her own country; the visit had been, whatever else, a school of theology. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch press the significance of her doxology: she "praised Jehovah his God, that out of His eternal love to His people Israel He had given them a king to do justice and righteousness," and they record that the earlier theologians, reading this confession together with our Lord's words (Matt. 12:42), inferred that the queen had been "converted to the true God."

The puritan Matthew Henry unfolds her blessing in three strokes. She blesses God as "the giver of Solomon's wisdom and wealth, and the author of his advancement"; she sees that God set him on the throne in kindness to him — "He delighted in thee" — recalling that Solomon's very name Jedidiah was given "because the Lord loved him" (2 Sam. 12:25); and she sees that God set him there in kindness to the people, "because the Lord loved Israel for ever." Above all she fastens on the end of his kingship: he was made king "to do judgment and justice." "He has made thee king," Henry paraphrases, "not that thou mayest live in pomp and pleasure, and do what thou wilt, but to do judgment and justice" — a truth she "kindly reminded Solomon of, and no doubt he took it kindly."

Henry even traces Solomon's prosperity to this, not to his wisdom merely: "bread is not always to the wise" (Eccl. 9:11), but "whoso doeth judgment and justice, it shall be well with him" (Jer. 22:15). The witnesses land together on the verse's great turn: a pagan queen, dazzled by gold and wisdom, looks through both to the God who gave them, and reads a king's magnificence as a trust for justice. Her theology outruns many an Israelite's — she blesses the Giver, not merely the gift, and remembers that thrones are given for the good of the ruled.
10:
The visit turns to the exchange of gifts: "she gave the king an hundred and twenty talents of gold, and of spices very great store, and precious stones." The reformed Gill observes that this was "the same sum that Hiram sent him" (compare 1 Kings 9:14), and that in it "was fulfilled the prophecy" concerning Solomon, that "to him shall be given of the gold of Sheba" (Ps. 72:15). The wesleyan Clarke reckons the bullion's worth in his own currency at more than eight hundred thousand pounds, adding that "the spices and precious stones might have been yet of more value" — and he notes in passing the textual oddity that verse 13, which closes the account of the queen, seems to have strayed from its place and would read more naturally straight after this verse.

The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the theology of the present. David had foretold "that to Solomon should be given of the gold of Sheba" (Ps. 72:15), and the gold and spices the wise men of the east would one day bring to Christ were, he says, "signified by this" (Matt. 2:11). "Thus she paid for the wisdom she had learned, and did not think she bought it dearly." Henry then turns the transaction upon the reader: "Let those that are taught of God give him their hearts, and the present will be more acceptable than this of gold and spices." He notes too the point the historian is careful to make — Solomon had "great abundance" of his own, and needed nothing she brought: "What we present to Christ he needs not, but will have us so to express our gratitude."

The witnesses do not divide. They read the queen's costly gift as at once the payment of a debt, the fulfilment of prophecy, and a type of a greater homage — the Gentile world bringing its treasures to the King. What she gives, Solomon does not need; what she has received, no gold could buy. The gift measures her gratitude, not Solomon's want, and points forward to wiser men from the east who would lay their gold before a greater than Solomon.
11:
The mention of the queen's gold draws the historian aside to another source of it: "the navy also of Hiram, that brought gold from Ophir, brought in from Ophir great plenty of almug trees, and precious stones." The reformed Gill reads the note as a quiet corrective — Solomon's wealth did not all come from Sheba, for "much" came "from Hiram, who fetched it from Ophir" (compare 1 Kings 9:28), and he gathers the ancient testimonies to the gold of India, where he places Ophir.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the almug (or algum) wood and its use. Of it Solomon made supports and possibly "a flight of steps or a staircase, so that we should have to think of steps with bannisters," for the temple and the palace, together with "guitars and harps for the singers." They weigh and reject the rival guesses of divans, panelled floors, and drawers as unsuited to the temple. The wesleyan Clarke canvasses the identity of the tree at length: the Vulgate's thyine wood or lignum vitae, the Septuagint's pine, the Syriac's cypress, the Arabic's "coloured wood" — "wood shaded of different colors, such as the rose wood" — and concludes that for "inlaying and veneering nothing can be finer than this wood."

The witnesses do not really dispute; they simply pool their knowledge around a rare cargo. What unites their comment is the recognition that this precious wood, like the queen's gold, flowed in to serve the two houses at the centre of the kingdom — the house of the LORD and the house of the king. The verse is a hinge: it explains where such treasure came from, prepares for the ascent and the instruments of the next verse, and quietly reminds the reader that behind Solomon's magnificence stood a far-flung commerce, a friendly alliance with Tyre, and — as Gill would have it — the providence that was pouring the wealth of the nations into Jerusalem. Even the almug trees, so admired that their like "came no more, nor were seen unto this day," become part of the record of a moment when the whole earth seemed to send its best to the house that bore the name of the LORD.
12:
The rare wood is now put to its use: "the king made of the almug trees pillars for the house of the LORD, and for the king's house, harps also and psalteries for singers." The reformed Gill takes the "pillars" to be rather "terraces" or a causeway — "the ascent or causeway he made from his own house to the temple" — and thinks it was the pavement, or the rails "on which men might stay themselves as they passed along." On this reading the almug wood built the very ascent "admired by the queen of Sheba," which is why the historian pauses to say what it was made of.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the supports as props or a balustraded staircase, and note the instruments made of the same wood — harps and psalteries "probably for the temple singers." They mark the historian's closing wonder: "there came no such almug trees, nor were seen unto this day," a cargo never matched before or since.

The witnesses do not divide over the substance, only over the exact article — whether pillars, terraces, a stair, or a raised walk. What they see together is a fitting destination for the costliest timber the ships had ever carried: it went not into private luxury but into the fabric that joined the palace to the sanctuary, and into the instruments that led the temple's praise. The wood that came from the ends of the earth was spent on the worship of God and the ascent to his house. It is of a piece with the whole portrait of Solomon at his height: the treasures of the nations, gold and gems and precious wood alike, gathered up and laid at the service of the temple. The queen had marvelled at the ascent by which the king went up to worship; the historian now tells her, and us, that even that was wrought of a wood the like of which the world would not see again. And the instruments made of the same timber carry the point further: the almug served both the going up to God's house and the singing of his praise within it, so that the rarest cargo of the reign was spent, first and last, on worship. What the earth sent, the temple received; the traffic of the nations ended at the altar.
13:
The account of the visit closes with royal generosity: "King Solomon gave unto the queen of Sheba all her desire, whatsoever she asked, beside that which Solomon gave her of his royal bounty. So she turned and went to her own country." The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the terms with care. They "indicate a position of inferiority, although well graced and honoured, in the queen"; her own present, he notes, "is of the nature of tribute," while Solomon gives "of his bounty" — both what she asked for, "probably by praising it," and "what else he would." Keil and Delitzsch gloss "according to the hand of the king" as the munificence answering to his greatness, the return-gift a monarch was bound in Eastern custom to make.

The reformed Gill states the fact plainly, that Solomon "gave unto the queen of Sheba all her desire." The wesleyan Clarke records the legend that grew up around the phrase: some imagine "she desired progeny from the wise king of Israel," and the Abyssinian traditions make her mother of a son, Menilek, who succeeded her and "introduced among his subjects the Jewish religion." Clarke reports it as tradition, not history, and the sober sense of "all her desire" remains the gifts and patterns and instruction she sought.

The witnesses do not divide, though Clarke's legend stands apart from the rest. What they hold in common is the picture of a giving that outdid the getting: the queen came laden and went away richer, for Solomon matched her tribute with a bounty of his own and denied her nothing she asked. The puritan Matthew Henry, closing the scene, cannot help lifting it to its type: "Thus those who apply to our Lord Jesus will find him not only greater than Solomon, and wiser, but more kind; whatsoever we ask, it shall be done for us; nay, he will, out of his divine bounty, which infinitely exceeds royal bounty, even Solomon's, do for us more than we are able to ask or think" (Eph. 3:20). The seeker who came from far returns satisfied — a small parable of every soul that brings its poverty to the true King and goes home enriched beyond its asking.
14:
With the queen gone, the historian totals the king's income: "the weight of gold that came to Solomon in one year was six hundred threescore and six talents of gold." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch caution that 666 is "evidently a round number founded upon an approximative valuation," and that it covers not merely taxes levied on Israel but "all the branches of Solomon's revenue" — commerce by sea and land, the royal domains, and the presents of foreign princes — the Ophir fleet's share arriving only "once in three years." The reformed Gill and the wesleyan Clarke both labour to render the sum in modern coin, Gill reckoning it near three millions of pounds, Clarke higher still, and Clarke adds that this annual bullion stood "independently" of the tribute of the Arabian kings and the duties of the merchants.

The puritan Matthew Henry alone pauses over the figure itself. He calls it "an ominous number," setting it beside the number of the beast (Rev. 13:18), and reads the whole inventory of Solomon's wealth with a wary eye — the apes and peacocks of the Tarshish fleet he suspects a sign that king and people, "being overcharged with prosperity, by this time grew childish and wanton." Yet Henry's chief concern is the use of it: Solomon "did not hoard it up," for the man who wrote so sharply against hoarding would not be guilty of it; God had given him "power to eat thereof, and to take his portion."

Above all Henry reads the sum against the man's own beginning and end. This "was he who, when he was setting out in the world, did not ask for the wealth and honour of it, but asked for a wise and understanding heart" — and had both added (compare 1 Kings 3:13); and this "was he who, having tasted all these enjoyments, wrote a whole book to show the vanity of all worldly things." The witnesses do not divide over the arithmetic; they divide only in tone — the historians counting the gold, the puritan weighing it. Together they set before us a king at the summit of earthly wealth, and remind us that the same king would call it all vanity, and that the moderate desire which asked first for wisdom is the surest way to have the rest added.
15:
The historian adds the streams that fed the great total: the yearly gold came in "beside that he had of the merchantmen, and of the traffick of the spice merchants, and of all the kings of Arabia, and of the governors of the country." The reformed Gill reads the first items as customs — what the traders "paid him as a duty or custom for the importation of their goods" — so that Solomon drew revenue not only from his own ventures but from the commerce that passed through his land.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the verse within the sum of the preceding: these were the additional branches of a revenue gathered from trade, tribute, and domain alike. Ellicott fixes on the last word, "governors." The term (pechah) is "of foreign origin," used constantly of foreign officers or satraps — of the Syrians (1 Kings 20:24), the Assyrians (2 Kings 18:24), the Persians (Esther 8:9) — and here it denotes not the officers over Israel described earlier but "governors (Israelite or foreign) in tributary countries." Ellicott even suspects it "a word of later origin than the age of Solomon, introduced by the compiler of the book."

The witnesses do not divide. They see in the verse the reach of Solomon's dominion: kings of Arabia sending their gifts, governors of subject provinces rendering their tribute, merchants paying their tolls, so that wealth flowed to Jerusalem from every quarter of a wide empire. The single point of scholarly interest — Ellicott's note on the foreign, perhaps later, word for "governors" — touches the history of the text, not its sense. What the passage records is plain enough: the 666 talents were only the visible crest of a revenue whose sources were many and whose tributaries were kings. The historian is building, item by item, the picture of a reign in which, as the queen had said, God had set a king over Israel whose greatness the surrounding nations were content to enrich. Gill's word "custom" and Ellicott's "tributary countries" meet in the same picture: a dominion so wide that its very traffic and its subject provinces alike paid into one treasury, and the wisest of kings sat at the centre of a commerce that drew the earth toward Jerusalem.
16:
Solomon begins to spend his gold on state: "King Solomon made two hundred targets of beaten gold: six hundred shekels of gold went to one target." The reformed Gill explains that these were "a larger sort of shields, which covered the whole body," made "of gold beaten with the hammer, or drawn into plates," as the Carthaginians too were said to make shields of gold. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reckon the six hundred shekels at about seventeen and a half pounds of gold to a single target — an extravagance of ornament that could only be for display. The wesleyan Clarke ventures that the tsinnah here "might resemble the Highland targe or target, with a dagger projecting from the umbo or center."

The puritan Matthew Henry supplies the meaning the others leave to the metal. These targets and the shields that follow were made "not for service, but for state, to be carried before him when he appeared in pomp." And he draws the contrast with other emblems of office: "With us, magistrates have swords and maces carried before them... But Solomon had shields and targets carried before him, to signify that he took more pleasure in using his power for the defence and protection of the good." Magistrates, he says, are "shields of the earth."

The witnesses do not divide. They see in the golden targets both the excess of a reign so rich that it clothed even its parade-armour in bullion, and — with Henry — a fitting sign of what royal power is for. The shield is a defensive weapon; a king whose ceremonial arms are shields rather than swords proclaims himself the guardian of his people rather than their terror. The gold that might have been hoarded is here worn openly, and the very ostentation is turned, in Henry's reading, into a parable of protective rule. Yet a reader who remembers the end of the story cannot forget that these same golden shields would one day be plundered, and cheap brass hung in their place — the first visible measure of a glory that did not outlast the house that made it.
17:
To the larger targets Solomon adds the smaller: "he made three hundred shields of beaten gold; three pound of gold went to one shield: and the king put them in the house of the forest of Lebanon." The reformed Gill marks these simply as "a lesser sort," the companion pieces to the great targets of the previous verse. The grammatical-historical Ellicott fixes the measure: the "pound" is the maneh, "equal to one hundred shekels" (compare 2 Chron. 9:16), so that each shield carried three minas of gold. Keil and Delitzsch count the whole array at five hundred pieces — two hundred targets and three hundred shields — all of beaten gold, and note that they were laid up in the great cedar armoury.

The wesleyan Clarke, ever the reckoner, computes the two hundred targets at some twenty-eight thousand pounds and the three hundred shields at more than two hundred thousand, a staggering outlay in ornamental arms. The puritan Matthew Henry keeps his eye on their purpose: like the targets, these were for pomp and not for the field, carried before the king to declare that his strength was spent on the defence of the good.

The witnesses do not divide. They agree on the metal, the measure, and the meaning, and together they register the scale of a reign in which even the ceremonial furniture of the court was cast in gold and stored by the hundred. The detail is not idle: the historian is showing how completely the wealth of the nations had come to rest in Jerusalem, so that gold was spent where lesser kings would have spent bronze. And the resting-place is itself significant — the house of the forest of Lebanon, the cedar hall that served as armoury and treasury alike, where these golden shields would hang until, a few reigns later, they were carried off and replaced with brass, a loss the history will one day record. For now they hang untarnished, the emblems of a kingdom at its zenith. The precise weights the witnesses so carefully reckon — a maneh of a hundred shekels to each shield, and thrice that to each target — are the historian's way of measuring the glory: he counts the gold not from avarice but to show a wealth so settled that it could be squandered on the mere trappings of state. What lesser reigns would have hoarded, Solomon's beat into ornaments and hung upon a wall.
18:
The chapter's most detailed marvel is the throne: "the king made a great throne of ivory, and overlaid it with the best gold." The reformed Gill reads the material typologically — the ivory being white "may denote the purity, justice, and equity with which he judged" — and hears in the great white throne of the Apocalypse (Rev. 20:11) an allusion to this seat of judgment; the ivory itself, he notes, came by the Tarshish fleet.

The grammatical-historical Ellicott gathers the history of ivory in Israel. It was brought in by the Tyrians, and the only other notice of it is Ahab's "ivory house" (1 Kings 22:39), Ahab too being allied with Tyre; the "ivory palaces" of the royal psalm belong presumably to this age (Ps. 45:8). The Tyrians drew it through Dedan in Arabia (Ezek. 27:15), whither it came from India, and perhaps from Africa as well. The throne, he thinks, was "probably inlaid with ivory and gold," the work of Tyrian craftsmen, and every feature carried a meaning: "the lion was the emblem of the house of Judah; the number twelve corresponded to the twelve tribes; and the exaltation of the throne... was the emblem of majesty," the more striking in a land where men commonly sat on the ground.

Keil and Delitzsch add the point of craft: the throne was "not made of ivory, but merely ornamented with ivory," the gold laid upon the wood and the ivory set within the gold. The puritan Matthew Henry sums the purpose — a "stately throne, on which he sat, to give laws to his subjects, audience to ambassadors, and judgment upon appeals." The witnesses do not divide. They read the throne as at once the height of Solomon's magnificence and a sermon in furniture: whiteness for equity, gold for glory, the lion for the royal tribe, the twelve for the nation, and the great ascent for the majesty of a judgment-seat — a throne meant to preach the character of the justice dispensed from it. Gill and Ellicott approach it from opposite ends, the one reading its whiteness inward toward the equity of the judge, the other reading its emblems outward toward the tribes he judged; but they meet in the same conviction, that Solomon's seat was no mere chair of luxury but the visible sermon of a kingdom whose glory was meant to serve its righteousness.
19:
The description grows precise: "the throne had six steps, and the top of the throne was round behind: and there were stays on either side on the place of the seat, and two lions stood beside the stays." The reformed Gill sees a practical purpose in the height — the six steps raised the seat "that everyone in court might see him, and the better hear the sentence he gave." The wesleyan Clarke pictures the shape from the Hebrew: a chair "round-topped" and "two-armed," like the ancient high-backed seats, and observes that the word rendered "stays" is literally yadoth, "hands" — the arms of the throne on which the king's own hands would rest.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch attend to the difficult phrase for the back, "round behind," comparing the variant in the Chronicles, and read the whole as a seat arched or rounded above, flanked by carved lions at the arm-rests. They let the account run on into the golden vessels of the next verses, but pause here on the lions that guard the seat.

The puritan Matthew Henry finds the throne's lesson in those lions. The arms and the steps alike were "supported by the images of lions in gold," set there "to be a memorandum to him of that courage and resolution wherewith he ought to execute judgment, not fearing the face of man" — for "the righteous, in that post, is bold as a lion" (Prov. 28:1). The witnesses do not divide. They read the six steps as majesty lifted up for all to see, the arms as the seat of a hand that steadies judgment, and the lions as the courage a judge must bring to the bench. Every carved detail preaches the same text: that the throne is not for display only but for justice, and that justice requires a king raised above fear as high as his seat is raised above the floor. What overwhelmed the queen was not merely the gold but the sight of power so ordered that its very furniture spoke of righteousness. Clarke's small philological note — that the "stays" are literally "hands" — deepens the picture: the king's hands rested where the carved lions stood, so that strength and judgment met at the very place his own grip closed upon the seat of power.
20:
The lions are numbered and the throne pronounced without peer: "twelve lions stood there on the one side and on the other upon the six steps: there was not the like made in any kingdom." The reformed Gill counts them exactly — "a lion on each side of every step" — and reads them as "a symbol of royal power," recalling that even "the Egyptians placed lions under the throne of Orus." Six steps, two lions to a step, made the twelve, and the number was not idle.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply register the historian's boast — "there was not the like made in any kingdom" — the throne standing unmatched among the seats of the earth's kings, as its maker stood unmatched among them, a claim the chapter will make outright of Solomon himself. The puritan Matthew Henry gathers the symbolism the others state: the golden lions, guarding steps and arms, set before the king at every ascent the courage that judgment demands, so that he could not mount his seat without being reminded to be "bold as a lion" for the right.

The witnesses do not divide, and the material is spare; but the verse rewards a moment's weight. Twelve lions for the twelve tribes of Israel turn the throne into an image of the nation it governed — each tribe, as it were, represented by a lion of gold, the royal beast of Judah multiplied across the whole people. The seat that no other kingdom could match was thus a seat that carried Israel in its very ornament, lifted six steps into view, flanked by the emblem of a strength that serves justice. The historian, having described it down to the last carved lion, steps back to say what the queen had felt: that nothing like it was to be seen in all the world. It is the visible summit of the glory that the next verses will translate into gold and silver and ships. Gill's link to the lions "under the throne of Orus" and Henry's reading of them as monitors of courage are not at odds; the same carved beasts that proclaimed a king's power to the nations preached to the king himself the boldness his office required. The throne that no kingdom could rival was, on every step, a summons to rule without fear and to judge without favour.
21:
Even the tableware turns to gold: "all king Solomon's drinking vessels were of gold, and all the vessels of the house of the forest of Lebanon were of pure gold; none were of silver: it was nothing accounted of in the days of Solomon." The reformed Gill accounts for the abundance by the "quantities" of gold "brought to him from Ophir, and paid to him in tribute, and given him as presents." The grammatical-historical Ellicott points to the Chronicler's parallel, that "the king made silver in Jerusalem as stones" (2 Chron. 9:27), and explains that the silver came in by the navy of Tarshish, its very plentifulness being the reason the historian pauses to mention that fleet. Keil and Delitzsch add the mechanism: silver "was counted as nothing, because the Tarshish fleet arrived once in three years," bringing it in floods.

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the verse two ways at once. First he lets Solomon enjoy his wealth without grudging: he "made all his drinking vessels, and all the furniture of his table, even at his country seat, of pure gold... he did not grudge himself what he had, but took the credit and comfort of it," for "that is good that does us good." But then he turns the despised silver into a sermon: "Such is the nature of worldly wealth, plenty of it makes it the less valuable; much more should the enjoyment of spiritual riches lessen our esteem of all earthly possessions." If gold could make silver seem as stones, "shall not wisdom, and grace, and the foretastes of heaven, which are far better than gold, make earthly wealth seem much more despicable?"

The witnesses do not divide. They see in the golden cups both the crest of Solomon's prosperity and its parable: a wealth so vast that the precious became common, silver reckoned at nothing beside the gold. Henry alone presses the moral, but it lies latent in the fact itself — that abundance cheapens what it multiplies, and that only the treasures of grace grow dearer, not cheaper, the more they are enjoyed.
22:
The historian names the source of the flood of gold and silver: "the king had at sea a navy of Tharshish with the navy of Hiram: once in three years came the navy of Tharshish, bringing gold, and silver, ivory, and apes, and peacocks." The greatest interest gathers, as often, on a single name — Tharshish — and here the witnesses openly work at a real puzzle. The grammatical-historical Ellicott lays it out most fully. On the one hand, "the Tarshish of Scripture is properly Tartessus in Spain": it is reckoned among the sons of Javan (Gen. 10:4), closely joined to Tyre (Ezek. 27:12), reached by sea (Jonah 1:3), and famous for the silver that was this navy's chief import. On the other hand, "ships of Tarshish" had become "a technical phrase for ships of large size," so that "a navy of Tarshish would not necessarily mean a navy going to Tarshish" — and the cargo here points east, not west: the peacocks "expressly indicate India," the word for ape (koph) resembles the Sanscrit kapi, and that for peacock the Tamil name. His solution is bold — "a circumnavigation of Africa by fleets from Tyre to Ezion-geber," the very voyage Herodotus records under Pharaoh-Necho, which alone would explain the emphatic "three years."

The reformed Gill reaches east by a shorter road, taking "Tharshish" not for a western port but for the sea itself — "the Tarsic sea," the same Indian sea that washed Ophir — so that the two fleets, joined together, were bound for the same eastern lands. The wesleyan Clarke offers the reading more cautiously as one of several "probable conjectures," and notes only that the "apes" (kophim) were "probably a species of monkey rather than ape." Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the opening of the summary that will gather up all Solomon's greatness in the verses to come.

The witnesses divide, then, over geography, not doctrine: whether the navy sailed to Tartessus in the far west or to India in the far east, and whether "Tarshish" names a place or a class of ship. But they converge on what the fleet meant. Every three years it emptied the wealth and the wonders of distant worlds into Jerusalem — precious metal by the ton and, alongside it, the apes and peacocks that Matthew Henry read as the toys of a court grown "childish and wanton" with prosperity. The gold that made silver worthless and the curiosities that amused a sated court came up the same gangplank, and the historian sets them side by side without comment, leaving the reader to weigh the splendour and the danger together.
23:
The historian draws the summit in a single line: "so king Solomon exceeded all the kings of the earth for riches and for wisdom." The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch catch the force of the verb — Solomon "became greater, not was greater" — a greatness reached and now displayed, gathering into itself the wealth, the wisdom, and the revenue described from every side, so that "all the world" sought him as the earlier record had said (compare 1 Kings 4:34). The wesleyan Clarke, characteristically, turns the general statement into figures, reckoning the king's daily income from the yearly gold at thousands of pounds a day, "each day, week, and year, one with another."

The reformed Gill lifts the verse at once to its type: in this surpassing greatness Solomon "was an eminent type of Christ," who exceeds all for riches and for wisdom in a higher sense than any earthly reckoning can hold. The puritan Matthew Henry, closing his survey of the king's magnificence, sets two remembrances against the glory. This "was he who, when he was setting out in the world, did not ask for wealth and honour, but for a wise and understanding heart" — and had all the rest added (compare 1 Kings 3:13); and this "was he who, having tasted all these enjoyments, wrote a whole book to show the vanity of all worldly things."

The witnesses do not divide. They read the verse as the crown of the chapter and the peak of the reign: a king who outstripped every other king on earth in the two things men most prize. Yet each of them, in his own key, guards the height with a warning or a promise — Gill by pointing past Solomon to Christ, Henry by remembering the prayer that asked wisdom first and the book that called it all vanity. The greatness is real and God-given; but the witnesses will not let it stand alone, and they surround the world's richest king with the reminders that made him, at his best, the wisest. Keil and Delitzsch's small grammatical point — that Solomon "became greater," not "was greater" — carries its own warning: a height that is reached can also be lost, and the verb that marks the ascent leaves room for the descent the next chapter will trace. The witnesses read the summit soberly, knowing that the two things the king surpassed all others in, riches and wisdom, were the very things his later folly would squander.
24:
The world comes to Solomon's door: "all the earth sought to Solomon, to hear his wisdom, which God had put in his heart." The reformed Gill guards the sense against overstatement — by "all the earth" is meant "the inhabitants of it, and only them, and those the more principal," the leading men who "came from the several parts of it, hearing the fame of his wisdom, to know the truth of it, and to improve themselves by it." And he fastens on the last clause: the wisdom was "all of God, a peculiar gift of his," put into Solomon's heart and not achieved by his own labour.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the verse with the next, as the foreigners who came drawn by his wisdom "brought Solomon presents" — gold and silver vessels, court dresses, armour, spices, horses, and mules — "a rate year by year," so that the seeking of his wisdom and the swelling of his revenue were one movement. The queen of Sheba, they imply, was but the most famous of a stream.

The puritan Matthew Henry had already fixed the note in the queen's own visit: those who came "to hear his wisdom" came, at their best, to be instructed in the things of God, and their coming was an aggravation of the stupidity of those who will not seek God by a greater than Solomon (Matt. 12:42). The witnesses do not divide. They see the whole earth converging on one man, and they trace the wisdom that drew it, past the man, to God who gave it. The verse is the summary of which the queen was the illustration: a Gentile world beating a path to Jerusalem to hear a wisdom that was, in its source, not Solomon's but the LORD's — a foreshadowing of the nations that would one day come to the light of a greater King. Gill's careful qualification — "all the earth" means its principal inhabitants, not literally every soul — and his insistence that the wisdom "which God had put in his heart" was a peculiar gift, together guard the verse from flattery: the honour was Solomon's, but the glory was God's, and the throng at the king's door was in truth a throng drawn by the God who had made him wise. So even the tribute of the nations became, rightly read, a testimony to the Giver rather than the gift.
25:
The seekers do not come empty-handed: "they brought every man his present, vessels of silver, and vessels of gold, and garments, and armour, and spices, horses, and mules, a rate year by year." The reformed Gill reads the gifts as the courtesy of approach — presents brought "to recommend them, and introduce them into his presence," the customary passport of any who would come before an eastern king. The wesleyan Clarke reads them more strictly as tribute, and lingers over the list: it "shows us of what sort that tribute was" — bullion "probably in ingots," "garments of very rich stuffs," armour "for little of this kind was ever made in Judea," spices that "sold well," and horses and mules, the mule being "the most necessary animal for all the purposes of life."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that these annual gifts came from "foreigners out of all lands" who sought Solomon's wisdom, the presents answering to his greatness and repeated "year by year." The historian thus turns the homage of the nations into a fixed revenue. The puritan Matthew Henry gathers them among the tokens the neighbouring princes sent "to engage the continuance of his friendship," not from fear so much as from admiration of his wisdom.

The witnesses do not divide. Whether the gifts are read as courtesy or as tribute, they tell the same story: the whole earth not only came to Solomon but paid to come, so that his wisdom drew wealth as surely as a magnet draws iron. The catalogue itself — silver and gold, rich garments, weapons, spices, and beasts of burden — is a picture of an empire at peace and at its zenith, receiving the best of every land as the natural due of the wisest of kings. Behind the parade of presents the reader is meant to see, with Gill, the God who "put wisdom in his heart," and who was, through all this tribute, making good the promise that the king who asked first for understanding should have riches and honour besides. Whether Gill's courtesy-gift or Clarke's tribute better names the presents, the two readings meet in the same fact: the nations rendered their best to Solomon "year by year," a steady stream and not a single homage, so that the whole earth's giving became one of the settled revenues of his reign.
26:
The record turns to Solomon's military strength: "Solomon gathered together chariots and horsemen: and he had a thousand and four hundred chariots, and twelve thousand horsemen, whom he bestowed in the cities for chariots, and with the king at Jerusalem." The reformed Gill reads the muster as prudent foresight — kept "both for war; for though it was a time of peace, he provided against the worst, lest an enemy should come upon him suddenly, and when unprepared." The wesleyan Clarke simply refers the numbers back to the earlier notice of Solomon's stalls and horsemen (compare 1 Kings 4:26).

The grammatical-historical Ellicott reads the gathering in its larger meaning. The multiplying of chariots is "the sign of military conquest and extended empire," set here "in connection with the growth of commerce and wealth, as one of the powers which held Solomon's kingdom together." He cites Josephus on the use of these chariots and horsemen for "progresses of royal magnificence and pleasure," but insists "their chief use was, no doubt, military," the "chariot cities" being the fortified posts through Solomon's own dominions and the tributary lands. Keil and Delitzsch note that the verse repeats an earlier record (compare 1 Kings 9:19).

The witnesses do not divide, though a shadow falls across the verse that they do not all name. Gill reads the chariots as wise defence, Ellicott as the sinew of empire; but a reader of the Law remembers the warning that Israel's king should "not multiply horses to himself" (Deut. 17:16), a caution the next verses will make sharper still by tracing the horses to Egypt. For now the historian records only the strength: fourteen hundred chariots and twelve thousand horse, garrisoned in the chariot-cities and at the capital, the visible guarantee of a peace bought and kept by force. The glory of the reign has a martial edge, and the witnesses let it stand as both the security of the kingdom and — to the watchful — the first hint of a king beginning to lean on what the LORD had told his kings not to trust.
27:
The wealth overflows into hyperbole: "the king made silver to be in Jerusalem as stones, and cedars made he to be as the sycomore trees that are in the vale, for abundance." The reformed Gill reads it plainly as "an hyperbolical expression," the effect of "the vast quantity he received from Tarshish." The wesleyan Clarke sharpens the paradox: Solomon "destroyed its value by making it so exceedingly plenty," and he had cedar imported and planted so freely "that it became as common as the sycamore trees," the sycamore being a tree that "partakes of the nature of the fig tree, and of the mulberry." Keil and Delitzsch call it "a hyperbolical description" of enormous quantities of precious metal and costly wood, noting that the sycomores, now rare, once "abounded in the lowlands of Palestine."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott alone lets the shadow fall. This influx of wealth "is specially noted as enriching Jerusalem," the court and the capital, "probably without preventing the imposition of heavy burdens on the provinces." From that unequal enrichment he traces a line forward: "Hence the division of interest and allegiance manifested at the accession of Rehoboam." The prosperity that in the reign's early years reached to all Judah and Israel (compare 1 Kings 4:20) had become, by its end, a wealth "gathered by tribute, and by a commerce entirely in the hands of the king," which "would enrich only the Court and the capital," leaving "the general population impoverished and oppressed."

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the moral the fact suggests: "Such is the nature of worldly wealth, plenty of it makes it the less valuable." If gold could make silver seem as stones, spiritual riches should make earthly wealth seem cheaper still. The witnesses do not divide over the sense; but between them they hold the verse in two lights at once — the dazzling one, in which silver lies about Jerusalem like paving-stones, and the sober one, in which Ellicott hears already the murmur that would one day split the kingdom. Abundance in the capital, the verse quietly warns, is not the same as justice in the land.
28:
The horses are traced to their source, and the trade to its mechanism: "Solomon had horses brought out of Egypt, and linen yarn: the king's merchants received the linen yarn at a price." The reformed Gill notes at once the difficulty of conscience — the importing of horses from Egypt "seems contrary to the command" of the Law. The wesleyan Clarke makes the point sharper: the Egyptians were reputed "the first people who used horses in war," and "God had absolutely prohibited horses to be imported or used" (compare Deut. 17:16); "but in many things Solomon paid little attention to the Divine command."

On the obscure word rendered "linen yarn" (mikveh) the witnesses labour without full agreement. Clarke reports it "hard to be understood, if it be not indeed a corruption," and canvasses the options: the Vulgate and Septuagint make it a proper name, "from Coa, or Tekoa"; Bochart takes it of tribute, the king's merchants farming the customs "at a price"; others make it a string tying horse to horse, or, with Rabbi Solomon Jarchi, "a collection or drove of horses"; Houbigant emends it to "chariots." The grammatical-historical Ellicott judges "the introduction of this linen yarn seems to be an error," and reads the Hebrew as "a troop of the king's merchants obtained a troop of horses at a fixed price" — the horses brought up in caravans from the plains of Egypt, where they abounded (compare Isa. 31:1); though he too records the Septuagint's striking variant, "and from Tekoa," which would make the verse run smoothly.

The witnesses divide, then, over a single doubtful word — linen yarn, a troop, a place-name, or chariots — and the versions divide with them. But beneath the philology two things stand clear on which they do not divide. First, the fact: Solomon's horses came out of Egypt through a royal monopoly, his merchants handling the traffic "at a price." Second, the shadow: those horses came from the one land, and by the one means, the Law had marked out for a king to avoid. Gill and Clarke will not let the reader miss it. The magnificence of the chariots and the cavalry, so proudly numbered two verses before, rested on a commerce that ran back to Egypt against the plain word of God — a small disobedience at the height of the glory, and a seed of the greater declensions the next chapter will record.
29:
The chapter ends with prices and partners: "a chariot came up and went out of Egypt for six hundred shekels of silver, and an horse for an hundred and fifty: and so for all the kings of the Hittites, and for the kings of Syria, did they bring them out by their means." The reformed Gill reckons the six hundred shekels at some seventy-five pounds by his valuation of the coin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that "chariot" here means the vehicle "with the team of horses belonging to it," so that the round figure need not be simply four horses at a hundred and fifty each, since "the chariot itself would certainly not be given in." The wesleyan Clarke takes the sums as "the ordinary price" and identifies the buyers: the "kings of the Hittites" must be "the remains of the original inhabitants of Canaan," who had removed to Syria and "formed themselves into a principality there"; and he stresses the monopoly — "neither horses nor chariots came out of Egypt but by means of Solomon's servants."

The grammatical-historical Ellicott confirms and enlarges the point. The chariot is the vehicle and its team, the horse the single charger; the moderate price "shows that the supply was large, and the commerce regular." The Hittites and the kings of Syria were "evidently allies or tributaries of Solomon, who were allowed, or compelled, to purchase their horses and chariots through his merchants" — Solomon standing as the great broker of Egyptian cavalry to the whole region. And he notes a striking confirmation from outside Scripture: of all the earlier peoples of Canaan the Hittites alone are named as surviving "in power" (compare 2 Kings 7:6), and this "is curiously confirmed by both Egyptian and Assyrian inscriptions, describing a powerful confederacy of Hittites" in the valley of the Orontes — the same northern chariot-power met long before at the conquest (compare Josh. 11:4).

The witnesses do not divide. They read the closing verse as the ledger of a vast arms-trade: Solomon buying from Egypt and selling on to the Hittites and the Syrians, his servants the sole channel and his treasury the richer for every chariot that passed through. It is a fitting end to the chapter of his wealth — commerce, tribute, and horses all flowing through one royal hand. Yet the reader who has heard Gill and Clarke on the previous verse cannot forget where the horses came from. The reign closes its inventory at the summit of earthly greatness, and on the very trade the Law had warned against; the historian sets down the glory and the danger in the same breath, and passes, in the next chapter, to the sorrow.
